text: This is an elderly female with chronic biliary dyskinesia. She had an ejection fraction of seven percent, chronic pain, dull ache, right upper quadrant, was reluctant to undergo any surgery.
The surgeon encounters filmy adhesions near the right hepatic margin, with the duodenum visibly creeping up slightly in the surgical field.
The cystic duct becomes visible in the background as the surgeon establishes the critical view of safety, with the posterior branch of the artery also coming into view.
The surgeon identifies the cystic duct and cystic artery, with visible fat fusing the gallbladder laterally to the right hepatic margin. The narrow cystic plate is also observed.

Every gallbladder is different. That's why I think it's important to have a lot of videos. These videos are intended for medical students, but also for residents and junior attendings and even senior attendings.

You know, I always love to watch different videos. I mean, anyone can do an easy gallbladder, but you realize when you're in private practice that not all gallbladders are created equal. I mean, every one of these gallbladders is different, and if you've been in practice long enough, you know that that's true.
I can tell you when it's late at night or after a long day of cases, you can really appreciate and enjoy a nice narrow cystic plate.
